content:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

7 min read
Just now

--

In Geoguessr, players are given an image of a place and have to guess the exact location. Over the years, experienced players have developed heuristics for specific region recognition and have learned to rely on clues like vegetation, street signs, buildings, and even shadow placements to discern countries and specific regions. While multimodal LLMs are now capable of visual interpretation, it’s not always clear what visual clues the models are relying on, so I decided to run a Geoguessr-style experiment with them.
I only focused on urban locations and selected a total of 26 cities from the Global North and Global South from a Kaggle dataset of world cities. For each city, I took images from Google Maps Street View, by manually navigating to the city view and taking a screenshot. The vast majority of images had some kind of text or street signage available save for 2 images — one in the Global North dataset and one in the Global South one. Each LLM was prompted to guess the city, state/province, and country exactly once and the results were compared to a source-of-truth text file with all the correct answers. A point was given for every city, state/province and country guessed correctly, and since each dataset had exactly 13 images, each model could get a maximum of 39 points. The models could also get partial credit if only a part of the location was guessed accurately (ie. correct country and region but wrong city).
The models used for this were Claude Opus 4.8, GPT 5.5, and Gemini 3.5 Flash. The models were given the same exact prompt with no few shot examples or chain of thought instructions. The outputs were captured via a structured json that had the model’s answer for city, state/province, and country as well as what visual features it relied on to make its guess. Each model got a single attempt per image to correctly guess the location with no retries for wrong answers. All models ran in parallel per image so no model saw another’s output. In order to avoid aliases from being marked incorrectly, a canonical id system was set up to map each location name to common alternate aliases and spellings. A potential limitation with this set up is that the models were run at default temperature settings, so deterministic evaluation cannot be assumed. While this does mean that results may not be reproducible, it helps with evaluating the models’ default visual reasoning behaviors.

Image used for RiyadhFor the global north dataset, all models got a score of 38/39, with Hong Kong’s identification being the common mistake. While all the models identified the image correctly as the city of Hong Kong, Gemini and Claude identified the state/province down as Kowloon and GPT identified the country as China. For the global south dataset, Gemini scored a 39/39, GPT scored a 35/39, and Claude scored a 33/39. There were four cities in this image set where at least one model got at least one part of the answer wrong: Guangzhou, Riyadh, São Paulo, and Karachi. Both GPT and Claude mistook the image of Guangzhou as Shenzhen, and Claude misidentified an image of Riyadh as Dammam and an image of São Paulo as Mexico City. When given an image of Karachi, GPT misidentified it as Muscat.
Based on the features it provided in its output, Claude seemed to most consistently rely on script & language, traffic direction, specific named text/landmarks, public transit vehicles and architecture style. The architectural style was interesting because Claude consistently noted in it almost every image and was generally very accurate. However, while it seemed to be very good at providing specific architecture styles especially for European cities, it seemed to struggle with naming buildings outside of iconic landmarks. As an example, when given an image of Sao Paulo, the building in the image that Gemini identified as the WTorre JK skyscraper, Claude identified it as a “modern glass skyscraper architecture”. On the other hand, GPT seemed to rely on naming specific descriptors, whether it was street signs or specific buildings along with a lot of the same visual cues that Claude consistently relied on. It identified the London Trocadero signage near Piccadilly Circus, the Mexico City style 55 phone number and the Jogno Tower in Seoul. Like Claude, GPT also used architecture style as a visual cue. However, unlike Claude, which used it as a broad descriptor, GPT used it to narrow in on specifics. In terms of features, Gemini was arguably the most reliant on named specific buildings and landmarks and hyper specific street detailing. What seemed to help Gemini the most was that it was able to accurately identify both well-known and lesser known buildings.
Part of Claude’s repeated failures seem to be explained by its dependency on iconic landmarks. When given an image with recognizable buildings, the model guesses the location accurately, but fails to do the same when it can’t identify specific features. This is seen when the model misidentified an image of Riyadh. Although the model correctly identified the image as a Saudi City, it seemed to be unable to identify any city-level cues so it identified the city as Dammam. The same thing was seen with its misidentification of Guangzhou as Shenzhen, where Claude again failed to identify specific buildings from the skyline. Claude’s most notable failure was São Paulo, where it mistook the Portuguese road signs for Spanish ones and then hallucinated Mexican license plates to reinforce its identification as Mexico City. This example suggests that Claude may have a relatively weaker self-correction mechanism. Once it decides that it has the right answer, it seems to fit all features to reinforce it. This behavior works well when Claude has the correct initial guess, but can be catastrophic when it has the wrong initial guess. GPT’s biggest failure was its misidentification of Karachi as Muscat. The image used was of Karachi DHA, which is a posh neighborhood known for its upscale infrastructure that, in some areas, can feel somewhat similar to Gulf residential developments. This might explain why GPT cited “modern white villa-style residential buildings” and “hot arid Gulf climate” as its reasoning for why it identified the city as Muscat. But it missed the Pakistani specific models of cars seen in the image. Unlike Claude, it didn’t hallucinate features, but this particular misidentification makes me wonder how it would behave given more images of ambiguous neighborhoods in well known cities. Similarly, Gemini’s hyperspecific landmark recognition approach worked really well for it, but it remains to be seen how it would perform with more suburban settings that don’t have readily recognizable landmarks and buildings.
Hong Kong was the only global north city that was misidentified, but its reasons for misidentification were taxonomical. Of all models, Claude had the largest scoring gap between global north and global south cities, with a gap of 5 points, while GPT had a scoring gap of 3 points and Gemini had a higher score in the global south dataset. On average, global south locations were more misidentified than global north locations, but this pattern appears to be driven by specific cities than regional patterns. Rather than underperforming in a single region, Claude seems to underperform with cities that don’t neatly fit into European or East Asian clusters, specifically São Paulo and Riyadh. South Asia as a region had the most variation in results. GPT completely missed Karachi and guessed Muscat instead and both Claude and GPT missed Guangzhou for Shenzhen. This appeared to be because both models lacked the specific building knowledge to distinguish the two cities’ skylines. Meanwhile, Gemini correctly identified three specific landmarks of the city. It is worth noting that Claude was significantly more specific with architecture styles for European countries, identifying “Haussmannian style apartments with wrought-iron balconies” in Paris, “classical Portland stone architecture” in London, and “monumental Stalinist neoclassical architecture” in Moscow. Claude continued to identify architecture, it seemed to generally be limited to more vague descriptors without using the full specificity it used for European cities.
Going forward, there are a couple improvements I would make for future iterations of this experiment. The most straightforward one is increasing the dataset size to include more images which would likely produce more robust results and reduce the impact of any single image on the model’s score. It would also be interesting to see how the results change beyond urban images and what features the models would rely on when given more suburban and rural terrains. More targeted datasets based on the behaviors seen would also help with testing out some of the inferences made here. For example, it would be useful to test GPT on more visually ambiguous neighborhoods and Claude on a wider range of images featuring distinct architectural styles to see if its identification of them varies based on regional patterns.
